1: ala hamefitz lefanayikh — "The scatterer has come up before your face." Ch 2 opens with the battle for Nineveh dramatized in present tense. The mefitz (scatterer/disperser) is the attacking nation coming against Nineveh — possibly the Babylonian-Median coalition that destroyed Nineveh in 612 BCE. The ironic command to Nineveh to "man the ramparts, watch the road, brace your loins, collect all your strength" is a taunt — Nineveh, who scattered other nations, now faces a scatterer itself.
2: ki shav YHWH et-ge'on Yaakov — "For YHWH is restoring the majesty of Jacob as the majesty of Israel." The theological grounding for the attack on Nineveh: YHWH is restoring the vine strippers have destroyed. The ge'on Yaakov (majesty/pride of Jacob) is what the Assyrians have emptied (beqequm) and destroyed — YHWH's restoration of his people is the reason for Nineveh's judgment.
3: magen gibboreihu me'adem — "The shield of his warriors is red; the soldiers are clothed in scarlet." The battle scene in vivid color: red shields (perhaps painted or reflecting firelight), scarlet uniforms, the flash of chariots like lightning on the day of preparation (yom hekino). The imagery is of an irresistible military force advancing — the very kind of army Assyria had deployed against others, now deployed against Nineveh itself.
4: be-chuzot yitholalun ha-rekev — "The chariots race madly through the streets." The chaos of urban warfare: chariots rushing through the city's open places (rechov), appearance like torches (lapidim), running like lightning. The city that once sent its war machines across the ancient near east now has those same machines — or their equivalent — turning its own streets into a battlefield.
5: yizkur addirav — "He summons his officers." Nineveh's commander summons his nobles (addirim); they stumble in their march (yikashelu be-halichatam). The protective covering (masakh) is hastily set up. The distinction between the confident advance of the attacker (vv. 3-4) and the stumbling defense of Nineveh's commanders (v. 5) enacts the reversal.
6: sha'arei ha-neharot niftachu — "The gates of the rivers are opened, and the palace melts away." This verse likely refers to the actual historical flooding of the Tigris and Khosr rivers that breached Nineveh's defenses in 612 BCE — ancient sources confirm this. The river-gates (sha'arei ha-neharot) opened by flood water turned the city's great defensive work into a liability. The palace (heikhal) "melts" (mumag) — dissolves under the assault.
7: vehutzav gilleta he'eleta — "It is fixed: she is stripped, she is carried away." Hutzav (it is fixed, determined, settled) — the decree is already issued. The feminine subject (Nineveh personified) is unveiled (gilleta) and led into exile. Her maidservants groan like doves, beating on their breasts — the mourning gesture of complete devastation. The city that stripped others is itself stripped.
8: ve-Nineveh kimeve mayim hi — "Nineveh is like a pool of water throughout her days." The comparison may describe Nineveh's population as fluid — always escaping — or her water-based defenses. "Stand! Stand!" the cry goes up, but no one turns back. The city that relied on numbers and loyalty finds both dissolving at the moment of crisis.
9: buzu kesef buzu zahav — "Plunder the silver, plunder the gold!" The conquerors are exhorted (or the text describes) the looting of Nineveh's treasury. Ein qetzeh la-tekunah — "there is no end to the treasure, an abundance of every precious thing." The irony: Nineveh's wealth, accumulated through generations of imperial tribute and conquest, is now scattered in a day.
10: buqah umevuqah umevullaqah — "Emptiness, void, and waste!" Three rare words in sequence (buqah, mevuqah, mevullaqah — possibly a sound-sequence intentionally mimicking desolation) describe Nineveh's total destruction. Hearts melt (namas lev), knees knock, anguish in every side, faces all grow pale (pnei kulam qibbtzu farim). The physical symptoms of terror in the survivors.
11: ayeh me'on arayot — "Where is the den of the lions?" The lion metaphor for Nineveh and its kings is now deployed in reverse. Assyrian kings hunted lions and used lion imagery extensively in their royal iconography — the lion was the symbol of royal power. Now Nahum asks: where is the lion's den, the pasture of the lion cubs? The lion that killed prey for his cubs (the Assyrian army feeding its dependent nations) — where is it now?
12: aryeh toreaf bede gurorav — "The lion tore enough for his cubs." The past tense marks the end: the lion used to tear prey and fill his caves with kill, his lair with torn prey. The imperial predator that sustained itself through violence against other nations has been exposed for what it always was — a predator, not a provider. YHWH's indictment of the empire is its own self-description turned against it.
13: hineni eleyikh ne'um YHWH tzva'ot — "Behold, I am against you, declares YHWH of hosts." The divine self-declaration against Nineveh. Three acts: burning her chariots in smoke, the sword devouring her lion cubs, cutting off her prey from the earth, silencing the voice of her messengers (mal'akheyikh). The last item is significant: Nineveh's messengers (who carried tribute demands and ultimatums across the empire) will be heard no more. The voice of imperial intimidation is silenced.
